main_summary.summary: Evans Derrell Hopkins, born 1954 in Danville, Virginia, recounts a trajectory from segregation-era childhood through Black Panther Party service to post-movement writing. He grew up in the Bruce Street neighborhood in a house his grandfather assembled brick by brick after being barred from buying on white-only West Main Street. His father Daniel Hopkins was Dan River Mills's first Black supervisor and ran a Danville landscape business. Hopkins was nine in 1963 when mass civil rights demonstrations against segregated buses, stores, and the Danville library brought wounded protesters into High Street Baptist Church and produced trials before Judge Archibald M. Aiken, who placed a pistol on the bench. He entered desegregated George Washington High School, led a 1970 student walkout demanding African American studies, and joined the BPP first as a community worker in Winston-Salem and then in 1972 as a newspaper writer in Oakland under editor David Graham Du Bois. He covered Bobby Seale's 1973 Oakland mayoral campaign and Elaine Brown's City Council run, met artists including Abbey Lincoln and photographer Stephen Shames, and worked alongside Emory Douglas. He left the party in 1974 amid Huey Newton's deterioration and internal disciplinary violence, was later imprisoned for armed robbery, and wrote his memoir Life After Life: A Story of Rage and Redemption.
key_themes: Black Panther Party national organization (Oakland editorial cadre)
Public school desegregation and student walkouts
COINTELPRO surveillance and disruption of Black radical organizing
Mass incarceration as continuation of civil rights struggle
main_summary.historical_significance: Hopkins provides a first-person inside account of the BPP's Oakland editorial operation during the 1972-74 transition from armed-struggle posture to electoral politics and community survival programs. His description of David Graham Du Bois as newspaper editor-in-chief, of the Bobby Seale mayoral campaign mechanics, and of the disciplinary culture in the party's final coherent years documents organizational history that has been undertold relative to the BPP's earlier Oakland founding period. The interview also preserves Danville-specific civil rights history: the 1963 demonstrations, the High Street Baptist Church first-aid role, Judge Archibald M. Aiken's courtroom pistol, and the post-integration removal of furniture from the Danville library as a final segregation tactic. Hopkins's reflections on COINTELPRO's documented surveillance of him personally, on the post-Panther turn toward armed robbery and incarceration, and on his memoir Life After Life: A Story of Rage and Redemption position him as a primary source for the long arc connecting Black radical organizing, federal counterintelligence, and the carceral system.

chapters.title: Danville Childhood and Family
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes his 1954 birth at Winslow Hospital, the segregated Black-only facility in Danville, Virginia. He details his mother Margaret Swanson Hopkins, his father Daniel Hopkins (Dan River Mills's first Black supervisor and founder of a Danville landscape business), and his maternal grandfather, who built houses on Bruce Street and physically moved a brick house from white West Main Street back to the Black neighborhood. Hopkins frames family complexion differences as a window into internalized racial hierarchy.
related_events: Black-owned construction and business in Danville Virginia

chapters.title: 1963 Danville Demonstrations
chapters.summary: Hopkins reads from his memoir to describe the 1963 mass demonstrations in Danville against segregated buses, department stores, and the city library. He recalls wounded protesters staggering into High Street Baptist Church for first aid, fire hoses and dogs deployed by police and deputized garbage men, and arrests tried before Judge Archibald M. Aiken, who placed a pistol on the bench. After courts mandated library integration, officials removed all tables and chairs to prevent Black patrons from sitting alongside whites.
related_events: Mass arrests and trials in Danville 1963

chapters.title: Integrating George Washington High
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes his family's decision to enroll him in newly desegregated George Washington High School in Danville rather than the distant county school. He recounts being harassed, jostled, called slurs, and once attacked from behind, and reflects in retrospect that the experience qualifies as racial bullying. He frames that period as foundational to his later rage and resistance.
related_events: Token integration of formerly white high schools

chapters.title: Discovering the Panthers
chapters.summary: On a 1970 trip to Washington D.C. Hopkins encountered the Black Panther Party newspaper through a cousin who sold it. He had already been wearing dashikis and an afro as part of the cultural nationalist movement, but the BPP offered him both racial and class analysis. He describes the fear permeating the Black community under Ku Klux Klan terrorism and the family practice of keeping window drapes closed.

chapters.title: Student Walkout at GW High
chapters.summary: Hopkins recounts organizing a 1970 walkout at George Washington High School to protest the absorption of historically Black John M. Langston High School into a middle school and to demand African American studies and a new school name. The walkout was leaked a day early, drew about 150 students including white participants, and resulted in Hopkins and other organizers being suspended for three days. He links the absence of police violence partly to the white students' presence and to the Kent State backdrop.

chapters.title: Winston-Salem Community Worker
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes moving to Winston-Salem at 17 to live with the BPP chapter as a community worker, the membership classification used after Huey Newton shut down national membership and David Hilliard managed the party. He worked sixteen-to-seventeen-hour days selling newspapers and running the free breakfast program. He recalls the dialectical materialism textbooks and the philosophical training in the party's socialist framework.
related_events: BPP membership freeze under David Hilliard
Survival programs serving the Black community

chapters.title: Newton Return and Cleaver Split
chapters.summary: Hopkins recounts joining the party in 1971 after Newton's prison release and the split with Eldridge Cleaver, whose New York chapter and international faction pushed armed struggle while Newton called for putting the gun away and emphasizing community survival programs. Hopkins still privately romanticized armed struggle. He frames BPP self-defense in the context of COINTELPRO and roughly thirty Panther deaths by 1971-72, including George Jackson at San Quentin.
related_events: George Jackson death at San Quentin 1971

chapters.title: Oakland Newspaper Cadre
chapters.summary: In August 1972 Hopkins moved to Oakland and joined the BPP newspaper editorial cadre under editor-in-chief David Graham Du Bois, son of W.E.B. Du Bois and stepson via Shirley Graham Du Bois. He worked alongside artist Emory Douglas and covered Bobby Seale's mayoral campaign and Elaine Brown's City Council race as the writer-and-sound-man for campaign tape recordings. Hopkins met photographer Stephen Shames, singer Abbey Lincoln, and filmmaker Bert Schneider, who was shooting a Seale campaign documentary.
related_events: Elaine Brown Oakland City Council campaign

chapters.title: Mentorship Under Du Bois
chapters.summary: Hopkins details how David Graham Du Bois, who had edited the English-language daily in Cairo for twelve years, taught him the fundamentals of journalism and the sensibilities of literary craft. Du Bois was working on a novel, And Bid Him Sing, set in Cairo around Malcolm X's hajj. Hopkins frames Du Bois as the mentor who shaped his cinematic eye, his sense of style, and his understanding of art as a vehicle for political truth.
related_events: David Graham Du Bois editorship of BPP newspaper
Publication of And Bid Him Sing

chapters.title: Party Crumbling 1973-74
chapters.summary: After Seale's 1973 mayoral loss in the runoff, the party began to unravel. Hopkins describes the disciplinary use of floggings, the climate of fear inside the organization, Huey Newton's increasing use of cocaine and cognac, Newton's 1977 pistol-whipping of his tailor, and Newton's later flight to Cuba after shooting a woman. Hopkins recounts stealing a car to fund bringing his pregnant fiancee from Winston-Salem to California, being disciplined within the party, and leaving Oakland the same weekend Newton fled to Cuba.

chapters.title: Women in the Party
chapters.summary: Hopkins reflects on the BPP's stated ideal of gender equality, the estimated forty-eight percent female membership in Oakland, and women's positions of leadership on the Central Committee. He frames Black women's broader role in the civil rights movement in terms of educational attainment and exposure to white households as a route to cultural enrichment. He briefly assesses Bobby Seale's character as organizer, jazz drummer, stand-up comedian, and sheet-metal mechanic.

chapters.title: COINTELPRO and Mass Incarceration
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes his post-Oakland turn toward armed robbery, a ten-year sentence, and a subsequent life sentence handed down by an all-white Danville jury for a robbery in which no one was injured. He analyzes the drug war and the cultural shift from Panther-era resistance to gang-versus-gang violence, framed via the documentary Bastards of the Party. He situates mass incarceration as a successor civil rights issue.
related_events: Hopkins's bank robbery conviction and ten-year sentence
Hopkins's life sentence in Danville Virginia

chapters.title: Heritage and Maggie L. Walker
chapters.summary: Hopkins discusses cultural justice alongside economic justice, the redistribution-of-wealth ideal he absorbed in the party, and the Tea Party movement's pull on working-class whites. He describes the Maggie L. Walker Governor's School in Richmond, which now occupies the formerly segregated Black high school named for the Richmond banker who founded the Independent Order of Saint Luke and the oldest surviving Black bank. He is writing a play and possible film about Walker.
related_events: Maggie L. Walker founding of Black bank Richmond Virginia
Maggie L. Walker Governor's School Richmond

chapters.title: COINTELPRO Personal Targeting
chapters.summary: Hopkins describes how COINTELPRO seeded distrust inside the BPP via poison-pen letters, helped imprison Elmer 'Geronimo' Pratt, and split Eldridge Cleaver from Bobby Seale. He recounts learning that COINTELPRO operatives went to Winston-Salem State University and blocked the scholarship he was up for there, citing him as a Panther. He frames the BPP as the philosophical extension of Malcolm X and closes on the power of love within the party as its enduring legacy.

loc_video.caption: Evans Derrell Hopkins oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Richmond, Virginia, 2011 July 07
